section: 5. What rights do I have when it comes to my data?
original_text: Right to data portability: You have the right to receive your data in a structured, commonly used, and machine-readable format. You can also request that we transfer the data directly to a third party, if technically feasible.
change_description: The entire 'Right to data portability' paragraph has been removed from Section 5.
rationale: RCASR3 tests constraint coverage: every constraint in the regulatory document must have a counterpart in the realization. Art. 20 GDPR grants data subjects the right to receive their personal data in a structured, machine-readable format and to transmit it to another controller. The original Hetzner policy covers this right explicitly. In the modified policy, the paragraph is deleted entirely, meaning the GDPR Art. 20 constraint has no counterpart in the realization. This deviation is detectable at the retrieval stage: the GDPR constraint(s) for Art. 20 will appear in unmapped_gdpr.json after re-running embed_and_match.py on the modified policy.

section: 5. What rights do I have when it comes to my data?
original_text: If, for example, you dispute the accuracy of your data or believe that the processing is unlawful, you can request that we restrict the processing of your data.
modified_text: If, for example, you dispute the accuracy of your data or believe that the processing is unlawful, you must submit a written request by post to our data protection officer to restrict the processing of your data.
change_description: The HOW of exercising the restriction right has been changed: from a simple informal request (any channel) to a mandatory written postal submission.
rationale: RCASR5 (execution style deviation) occurs when the responsibility part and the data part of a constraint pair match, but the task part — referring to HOW something must be done — deviates. Here: responsibility (data subject = 'you') is unchanged; data ('your data') is unchanged; but the task differs. The GDPR (Art. 18) requires only that the data subject assert the right — it imposes no formal channel requirement. The modified policy adds a burdensome procedural requirement (written post) that conflicts with GDPR Art. 12(2), which obliges controllers to 'facilitate the exercise of the data subject's rights' and not create unnecessary obstacles.

section: 5. What rights do I have when it comes to my data?
original_text: You can request the deletion of your data if it is no longer required for its original purpose.
modified_text: You cannot request the deletion of your data solely on the grounds that it is no longer required for its original purpose.
change_description: The erasure right has been negated: 'can request' has become 'cannot request', directly contradicting the data subject's right under GDPR Art. 17(1)(a).
rationale: RCASR6 (negation deviation) occurs when a constraint pair has similar responsibility, task and data parts, but the task in one document is negated relative to the other. Here: responsibility (data subject) and data (deletion of personal data) remain the same between the GDPR constraint and the policy sentence. However, the task is inverted — GDPR Art. 17(1)(a) grants the right to erasure when data is no longer necessary for its original purpose, while the modified policy explicitly denies this ground for deletion. This is the clearest possible negation deviation and would score negation similarity near -1 in the RCASR6 definition (sim(c_d|task, c_r|task) negative due to lexical negation).

section: 5. What rights do I have when it comes to my data?
original_text: If you would like to make use of any of these rights, please contact our data protection officer directly.
modified_text: If you would like to make use of any of these rights, please contact our customer service team directly.
change_description: The responsible party for handling data subject rights requests has been changed from 'data protection officer' to 'customer service team'.
rationale: RCASR7 (responsibility deviation) occurs when the task part and the data part of a constraint pair match, but the responsibility part — referring to WHO executes the task — deviates. Here: task (contact to exercise rights) and data (personal data rights) are unchanged. The deviation is in the responsible party: GDPR Art. 12(2) requires controllers to facilitate data subject rights, and Art. 37–39 establish the Data Protection Officer as the legally mandated designated contact for data subject rights requests. Replacing 'data protection officer' with 'customer service team' misidentifies the legally required resource.

section: 5. What rights do I have when it comes to my data?
original_text: If the data we have stored about you is incorrect or incomplete, you can request its correction or completion.
modified_text: If the account information we have stored about you is incorrect or incomplete, you can request its correction or completion.
change_description: The data category covered by the rectification right has been narrowed from 'data' (implying all personal data) to 'account information' (a subset).
rationale: RCASR8 (data deviation) occurs when responsibility and task parts of a constraint pair match, but the data part — referring to WHAT is being acted upon — deviates. Here: responsibility (data subject / Hetzner) and task (request correction or completion) are unchanged. The deviation is in scope of data: GDPR Art. 16 grants the right to rectify 'inaccurate personal data' broadly, with no restriction to any data category. The modified policy limits this right to 'account information', which is a narrower subset (implying e.g. that rectification of data held in other contexts, such as marketing profiles or application files, would not be covered). This matches the RCASR8 definition: sim(c_d|data, c_r|data) falls below threshold because 'personal data' and 'account information' are semantically distinct categories.

section: 5. What rights do I have when it comes to my data?
original_text: Right not to be subject to automated decision-making: We do not make any automated decisions regarding your person. If we did, you would have the right not to be evaluated solely by algorithms.
change_description: The entire 'Right not to be subject to automated decision-making' paragraph has been removed from Section 5.
rationale: RCASR3 tests constraint coverage: every regulatory constraint must have a counterpart in the realization. GDPR Art. 22 grants data subjects the right not to be subject to solely automated decisions producing legal or similarly significant effects, and to obtain human intervention, express their views, and contest decisions. The original Hetzner policy acknowledges this right explicitly. In the modified policy, the paragraph is deleted entirely, meaning the Art. 22 GDPR constraint has no counterpart in the realization. This deviation is detectable at the retrieval stage: GDPR Art. 22 constraints will appear in unmapped_gdpr.json.

section: 6. Making complaints to the relevant data protection authority
original_text: If you believe that we are violating any data protection laws by processing personal data, you also have the right to make a complaint to the data supervisory authority who is responsible for our company.
modified_text: If you believe that we are not handling your data correctly, we encourage you to contact our data protection officer directly. We always strive to resolve any possible disagreements as quickly and smoothly as possible.
change_description: Section 6 has been rewritten to describe only an internal complaint process, removing any mention of the right to complain to the supervisory authority.
rationale: RCASR3: GDPR Art. 77 grants every data subject the right to lodge a complaint with a supervisory authority. The original Hetzner policy covers this right in Section 6. In the modified version, Section 6 has been changed to describe only an internal escalation process to the data protection officer, with no mention of any right to contact the supervisory authority. This means the Art. 77 regulatory constraint has no counterpart in the realization.

section: 5. What rights do I have when it comes to my data?
modified_text: We aim to process all data subject rights requests within 5 business days of receipt.
change_description: A new commitment has been added: Hetzner will respond to data subject rights requests within 5 business days, which is significantly stricter than the 1-month deadline allowed by GDPR Art. 12(3).
rationale: RCASR4 (severity deviation) occurs when a realization is over-compliant, meaning it is stricter than the corresponding regulatory requirement. GDPR Art. 12(3) allows controllers up to one month to respond to data subject requests, extendable by a further two months for complex or numerous requests. The modified policy commits to 5 business days (~1 calendar week), which is far stricter. This over-commitment creates an operational obligation not required by the regulation and may expose Hetzner to liability for failing to meet a self-imposed tighter deadline.

original_text: Log files are stored for a maximum of 30 days.
modified_text: Log files are stored for a maximum of 3 days.
change_description: The chatbot log file retention period has been reduced from 30 days to 3 days, imposing a stricter data retention limit than required by GDPR.
rationale: RCASR4 (severity deviation): GDPR Art. 5(1)(e) requires that personal data is kept no longer than necessary but does not mandate any specific minimum or maximum retention period for log files. The original policy already complied with Art. 5(1)(e) at 30 days. By reducing to 3 days, the modified policy is over-compliant: it imposes a stricter storage limitation than the regulation requires. This may cause operational issues (insufficient time for security analysis) without any regulatory mandate.

original_text: Rejection or withdrawal of the application: Six months after the rejection or withdrawal of your application.
modified_text: Rejection or withdrawal of the application: Four weeks after the rejection or withdrawal of your application.
change_description: The retention period for rejected application data has been reduced from six months to four weeks, which is stricter than any regulatory requirement.
rationale: RCASR4 (severity deviation): GDPR Art. 5(1)(e) requires storage limitation to what is necessary but sets no minimum retention period. Employment law typically justifies 6-month retention for rejected applications to defend against discrimination claims. The modified policy reduces this to four weeks — stricter than required, meaning Hetzner would delete data before it may be legally useful for defending against claims. This is over-compliance that creates practical legal risk.

original_text: You can withdraw your consent at any time to receive future newsletters by unsubscribing from the newsletter.
modified_text: You can withdraw your consent at any time to receive future newsletters by submitting a signed written request to our data protection officer by post.
change_description: The HOW of consent withdrawal has been changed from clicking an unsubscribe link to submitting a signed written postal request to the DPO.
rationale: RCASR5 (execution style deviation): GDPR Art. 7(3) requires that consent withdrawal must be 'as easy as giving it'. The original policy provides an unsubscribe link — as easy as the email subscription. The modified policy requires a signed written postal request to the DPO, which is far more burdensome than the original newsletter signup process. This directly violates Art. 7(3)'s requirement that withdrawal be as easy as giving consent, and also contradicts Art. 12(2) which obliges controllers to not create unnecessary obstacles to exercising data subject rights.

section: 5. What rights do I have when it comes to my data?
original_text: Right of access: You can request information at any time about whether and which personal data we process about you.
modified_text: Right of access: You can request information about your personal data by filing a formal request through our online customer portal at accounts.hetzner.com using your verified account login.
change_description: The HOW of exercising the right of access has been changed from any channel at any time to requiring a formal request through an authenticated customer portal.
rationale: RCASR5 (execution style deviation): GDPR Art. 15 grants data subjects the right to access their personal data without specifying any particular channel. GDPR Art. 12(2) obliges controllers to facilitate the exercise of rights. The original policy allows requests 'at any time', implying any contact method. The modified policy restricts this to an authenticated portal request, which excludes data subjects without a customer account (e.g., former customers) and imposes a specific technical process not required by the regulation. This is an execution style deviation: responsibility and data are unchanged but the HOW deviates.

original_text: Once these periods have expired, your data will be deleted.
modified_text: Once these periods have expired, your data will be archived in our long-term backup systems. We are not obligated to actively delete data from backup systems.
change_description: The obligation to delete data after retention periods has been replaced with archiving, and an explicit claim that no deletion obligation exists for backup data.
rationale: RCASR6 (negation deviation): GDPR Art. 5(1)(e) requires data to be 'erased or anonymised' when no longer necessary. GDPR Art. 17 grants the right to erasure. The original policy states 'your data will be deleted' after retention periods — a clear commitment to erasure. The modified version replaces deletion with archiving and explicitly negates the deletion obligation ('we are not obligated to actively delete data from backup systems'). This directly contradicts both Art. 5(1)(e) and Art. 17, which do not exempt backup systems from the erasure obligation.

original_text: In all other cases, the data will be deleted immediately after the verification is completed.
modified_text: In all other cases, the data will be deleted immediately after the verification is completed. We are not required to inform you if your identity verification data has been affected by a security incident, as iDenfy bears sole responsibility for such notifications.
change_description: A clause has been added explicitly negating the obligation to notify data subjects of security incidents affecting their identity verification data, redirecting responsibility to iDenfy.
rationale: RCASR6 (negation deviation): GDPR Art. 34 requires controllers to notify data subjects 'without undue delay' when a personal data breach is likely to result in a high risk to their rights and freedoms. The modified policy explicitly states that Hetzner 'is not required to inform' data subjects of security incidents involving their identity verification data and attributes sole notification responsibility to iDenfy. This negates Art. 34: as the data controller, Hetzner retains the notification obligation regardless of which processor experienced the breach.

original_text: We use the external service provider Brevo GmbH (Köpenicker Straße 126, 10179 Berlin, Germany) to manage and send the newsletter. We have signed a DPA with this provider.
modified_text: We use the external service provider Brevo GmbH (Köpenicker Straße 126, 10179 Berlin, Germany) to manage and send the newsletter. Brevo acts as an independent data controller for all newsletter-related processing, and Hetzner is not responsible for their data handling practices.
change_description: Brevo's role has been changed from a data processor operating under a DPA to an independent data controller, shifting responsibility away from Hetzner.
rationale: RCASR7 (responsibility deviation): GDPR Art. 28 requires controllers to use only processors that provide sufficient guarantees, implemented via a data processing agreement (DPA). Under the original policy, Brevo is correctly described as a processor acting under Hetzner's instructions via a DPA. The modified policy recharacterises Brevo as an 'independent data controller', removing Hetzner's processor oversight obligations. This misidentifies WHO is responsible for the newsletter processing and would, if true, change data subjects' rights (they would need to exercise rights against Brevo, not Hetzner, for newsletter data).

original_text: All further data collected by the provider is their own responsibility.
modified_text: All further data collected by the provider is their own responsibility. Any data subject rights requests regarding identity verification data must be directed to iDenfy directly, not to Hetzner.
change_description: Data subject rights requests regarding identity verification data have been redirected to iDenfy, misattributing the responsible party for handling GDPR rights.
rationale: RCASR7 (responsibility deviation): Under GDPR Art. 37–38, the Data Protection Officer and the controller are the designated contacts for data subject rights. Even where a processor (iDenfy) collects data on the controller's behalf, data subjects may exercise their rights against the controller (Hetzner). The modified policy instructs data subjects to contact iDenfy directly for rights requests about identity data, misidentifying WHO is responsible for responding to data subject rights requests. This WHO deviation could prevent data subjects from effectively exercising their rights.

section: 5. What rights do I have when it comes to my data?
original_text: Right to object: You can object to the processing of your personal data unless there are compelling legitimate reasons for the processing.
modified_text: Right to object: You can object to the processing of your marketing-related personal data unless there are compelling legitimate reasons for the processing.
change_description: The scope of the right to object has been narrowed from 'personal data' (all processing) to 'marketing-related personal data' only.
rationale: RCASR8 (data deviation): GDPR Art. 21 grants a broad right to object to processing based on legitimate interests or direct marketing, covering all personal data in those contexts. The responsibility (data subject) and task (object to processing) remain unchanged. However, the WHAT — the category of data the right covers — has been narrowed from 'personal data' (all categories) to 'marketing-related personal data'. This excludes the right to object to processing for other legitimate interests (e.g., fraud prevention, service improvement), violating the broader scope of Art. 21.

original_text: After you delete your forum account, your data will also be deleted immediately.
modified_text: After you delete your forum account, your username and email address will be deleted immediately. However, posts you have made in the forum will be retained in anonymized form.
change_description: The scope of data deleted upon forum account deletion has been narrowed: only credentials are deleted, while forum posts are retained in 'anonymized form' rather than fully deleted.
rationale: RCASR8 (data deviation): GDPR Art. 17 grants the right to erasure of personal data. The responsibility (Hetzner) and task (delete upon account deletion) remain unchanged. However, the WHAT is narrowed: the original policy deletes 'your data' (all personal data), while the modified policy only deletes the username and email address and explicitly retains forum posts. Forum posts may contain personal data and are not automatically rendered non-personal by anonymization. The deviation lies in the data scope: not all personal data is erased as the erasure right requires.
